questions.q: A liveness probe fails 3 times. What does Kubernetes do?
options: Removes the pod from Service endpoints only
Marks the pod as Unhealthy but keeps it running
Deletes the pod and creates a new one
questions.explanation: A failed liveness probe causes Kubernetes to kill the container and restart it (according to the pod's restartPolicy). The pod itself is not deleted — only the container is restarted, preserving the pod's IP and volumes.

questions.q: What is the difference between a liveness probe failure and a readiness probe failure?
options: Liveness removes from endpoints; readiness kills the container
Liveness kills and restarts the container; readiness removes it from Service endpoints without killing it
Both kill the container but readiness also deletes the pod
There is no difference — both have the same effect
questions.explanation: Liveness probe failure → container is killed and restarted. Readiness probe failure → pod is removed from Service endpoints so it stops receiving traffic, but it is NOT killed. This lets a container temporarily stop serving traffic without being restarted.

questions.q: Which restartPolicy should you use for a Kubernetes Job that should only retry on failure?
questions.explanation: OnFailure restarts the container only if it exits with a non-zero exit code. Always would restart even on clean exit, which is inappropriate for Jobs. Never would not retry any failure.

questions.q: A JVM application takes 3 minutes to start. Which probe prevents the liveness probe from killing it during startup?
options: startupProbe with failureThreshold × periodSeconds covering the startup time
No special probe — just increase periodSeconds
questions.explanation: The startupProbe disables the liveness and readiness probes until it succeeds. Setting failureThreshold: 30 and periodSeconds: 10 gives the app up to 5 minutes to start. Using a high initialDelaySeconds on liveness is fragile because it applies to every restart, not just the first start.

questions.q: Which probe check method succeeds when a command exits with code 0?
questions.explanation: The exec probe runs a command inside the container and considers it successful if the command exits with code 0. httpGet checks for 2xx/3xx HTTP responses, and tcpSocket checks if a port accepts a connection.
